answer: Argon2id is the password hashing algorithm used in production. Parameters include side-channel resistance hardening as mentioned in the security justifications, but specific values for time cost, memory cost, and parallelism are NOT IN CONTEXT.
NOT IN CONTEXT. The seed mentions RS256-tokens and JWT implementation but does not specify refresh token expiration period or rotation policy.

Argon2id was chosen over bcrypt because it provides stronger side-channel resistance. The seed explicitly lists 'Argon2id(side-channel)' as a key reason for the architecture decisions.

Drizzle is used instead of Prisma because it has zero external dependencies ('Drizzle(zero-deps)'), making it more suitable for the microservices architecture where dependency minimization is critical.

Both Portfolio and Notification services verify auth tokens by calling the shared Lambda-JWKS endpoint through the API Gateway. The flow is: auth service signs with RS256, other services fetch public keys from Lambda-JWKS for validation via the gateway.

Rate limiting is done at the ALB (Application Load Balancer) pre-container instead of in application code because it provides a defense layer before requests reach individual services, reducing load and preventing application-level DoS attacks.

When Portfolio Service validates a user's role before rebalance: Client request hits ALB with rate limiting > Lambda-JWKS verifies token signature > Portfolio Service receives validated JWT > RBAC-middleware checks role permissions from token claims > approval/denial returned.

If JWKS endpoint goes down, all three services (auth, portfolio, notify) are affected because they depend on JWKS for token validation. There is a grace period via LRU-cache-grace, meaning cached keys allow continued operation until cache expires, but on cache miss requests fail.
Real-time portfolio alerting (WebSocket) is listed as a 'next' feature (Q3 roadmap item), currently NOT IN CONTEXT as to dependencies or status.

Webhook notifications for auth events would be implemented using the existing webhook infrastructure mentioned in the seed. Implementation would involve: auth service emitting auth events > webhook service queuing via Celery > HTTP POST delivery with retry policy (specific retry details NOT IN CONTEXT).

The connection pool interacts with the Celery memory issue during tax-lot processing by potentially exhausting available connections when Celery workers attempt database access. The Celery-mem bug is noted as a current issue. Pool configuration details and resolution are NOT IN CONTEXT.

Fargate Spot cannot be used for Auth Service because it handles authentication and token signing (RS256-JWKS). Spot instances can be interrupted, causing service unavailability. The seed specifies 'Spot-batch-only', restricting Spot to batch workloads only.

MFA affects the login flow by adding an additional step: standard credential validation (Argon2id hash verification) > MFA-TOTP verification > RS256-token generation. MFA is listed as a recently added feature in the current state.

Credential security posture: At rest - AES256-GCM encryption per tenant ('AES256GCM-tenant'), Argon2id hashing for passwords in production. In transit - mTLS between services ('Envoy-mTLS'), sslmode-verify-full for database connections, RS256-JWT tokens signed by Lambda-JWKS and validated by all consumers.

Complete login with MFA: Client submits credentials > ALB rate limiting check > Fastify auth service receives request > Argon2id verifies password against PostgreSQL stored hash > MFA-TOTP prompt issued > User provides TOTP > Validation succeeds > RS256-JWT token signed > Token returned to client.

New Billing Service (Rust) must follow patterns: token validation via Lambda-JWKS > RBAC-middleware for authorization > RFC7807 error responses > mTLS with Envoy for inter-service communication > RDS-PG for data persistence > deployment via GHA>ECR>ECS-blue-green with ClaudeCode-review gates > Spot-batch restriction if batch processing.

1500 req/min from single IP to Auth Service: ALB rate limiting tier rejects excess requests per configured threshold > remaining requests route to Fastify auth service > if still over limits, individual service-level throttling engages > Envoy mTLS connection established for validated requests > PostgreSQL connection pool manages database queries.

JWT signing key (Lambda-JWKS) dependencies: Auth Service (generates), Portfolio Service (validates), Notification Service (validates), API Gateway (caches JWKS), all three Fastify/FastAPI/Chi services (consume), mTLS setup depends on key material, token generation flow depends on key availability.

Deployment pipeline: Developer commits code to GitHub > GHA workflow triggered > application built > Docker image pushed to ECR > ECS-blue-green deployment initiated across 3 AZ Fargate infrastructure > ClaudeCode-review gate enforces code review > mTLS and Envoy configs updated > RDS-PG and Redis caches verified > health checks confirm 3 AZ rollout success > traffic shifted from blue to green.
